Historic Centre of Avignon: [Papal Palace](/wiki/Palais_des_Papes "Palais des Papes"), Episcopal Ensemble and [Avignon Bridge](/wiki/Pont_Saint-B%C3%A9n%C3%A9zet "Pont Saint-Bénézet")

([/ˈævɪnjɒ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/ˌævɪnˈjoʊn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) French: [[aviɲɔ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Jules78120-Avignon.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jules78120-Avignon.wav"); [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Provençal dialect"): *Avinhon* (Classical norm) or *Avignoun* ([Mistralian norm](/wiki/Mistralian_norm "Mistralian norm")), IPA: [[aviˈɲun]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan"); [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Avenio*) is the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") of the [Vaucluse](/wiki/Vaucluse "Vaucluse") department in the [Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te_d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur") region of [southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"). Located on the left bank of the river [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône"), the [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") has a population of 92,188 (2023),[[8]](#cite_note-pophist-8) with about 16,000 (estimate from Avignon's municipal services) living in the ancient town centre enclosed by its [medieval walls](/wiki/Walls_of_Avignon "Walls of Avignon"). The [Communauté d'agglomération du Grand Avignon](/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_d%27agglom%C3%A9ration_du_Grand_Avignon "Communauté d'agglomération du Grand Avignon"), a cooperation structure of 16 [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France"), had 197,102 inhabitants in 2022.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

**Avignon** ([/ˈævɪnjɒ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/ˌævɪnˈjoʊn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) French: [[aviɲɔ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Jules78120-Avignon.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jules78120-Avignon.wav"); [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Provençal dialect"): *Avinhon* (Classical norm) or *Avignoun* ([Mistralian norm](/wiki/Mistralian_norm "Mistralian norm")), IPA: [[aviˈɲun]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan"); [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Avenio*) is the [prefecture](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") of the [Vaucluse](/wiki/Vaucluse "Vaucluse") department in the [Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te_d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur") region of [southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"). Located on the left bank of the river [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône"), the [commune](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") has a population of 92,188 (2023),[[8]](#cite_note-pophist-8) with about 16,000 (estimate from Avignon's municipal services) living in the ancient town centre enclosed by its [medieval walls](/wiki/Walls_of_Avignon "Walls of Avignon"). The [Communauté d'agglomération du Grand Avignon](/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_d%27agglom%C3%A9ration_du_Grand_Avignon "Communauté d'agglomération du Grand Avignon"), a cooperation structure of 16 [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France"), had 197,102 inhabitants in 2022.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Avignon is [France's 35th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Functional_area_(France) "Functional area (France)") according to [INSEE](/wiki/INSEE "INSEE") with 337,039 inhabitants (2020),[[3]](#cite_note-aav-3) and France's 13th-largest [urban unit](/wiki/Urban_unit "Urban unit") with 459,533 inhabitants (2020).[[2]](#cite_note-uu-2) Its urban area was the fastest-growing in France from 1999 until 2010 with an increase of 76% of its population and an area increase of 136%.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

Between 1309 and 1377, during the [Avignon Papacy](/wiki/Avignon_Papacy "Avignon Papacy"), seven successive [popes](/wiki/Pope "Pope") resided in Avignon and in 1348 [Pope Clement VI](/wiki/Pope_Clement_VI "Pope Clement VI") bought the town from [Joanna I of Naples](/wiki/Joanna_I_of_Naples "Joanna I of Naples"). Papal control persisted until 1791 when during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") it became part of France. The city is now the capital of the [Vaucluse department](/wiki/Vaucluse "Vaucluse") and one of the few French cities to have preserved its city walls. This is why Avignon is also known as '*La Cité des Papes*' (The City of the Popes).

The historic centre, which includes the [Palais des Papes](/wiki/Palais_des_Papes "Palais des Papes"), [Avignon Cathedral](/wiki/Avignon_Cathedral "Avignon Cathedral") and the [Pont d'Avignon](/wiki/Pont_Saint-B%C3%A9n%C3%A9zet "Pont Saint-Bénézet"), became a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1995 because of its architecture and importance during the 14th and 15th centuries.[[11]](#cite_note-unesco-11) The medieval monuments and the annual [Festival d'Avignon](/wiki/Festival_d%27Avignon "Festival d'Avignon") – one of the world's largest festivals for performing arts[[12]](#cite_note-12) – have helped to make the town a major centre for tourism.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The earliest forms of the name were reported by the Greeks:[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERostaing199430-13) Аὐενιὼν *Aueniṑn* (Stephen of Byzantium, Strabo, IV, 1, 11) and Άουεννίων *Aouenníōn* (Ptolemy II, x).

The Roman name *Avennĭo Cavărum* (Mela, II, 575, Pliny III, 36), i.e. "Avignon of Cavares", accurately shows that Avignon was one of the three cities of the Celtic-Ligurian tribe of [Cavares](/wiki/Cavares "Cavares"), along with [Cavaillon](/wiki/Cavaillon "Cavaillon") and [Orange](/wiki/Orange,_Vaucluse "Orange, Vaucluse").

The current name dates to a pre-Indo-European[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERostaing199430-13) or pre-Latin[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDauzatRostaing19631689-14) theme *ab-ên* with the suffix *-i-ōn(e)*.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERostaing199430-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDauzatRostaing19631689-14) This theme would be a [hydronym](/wiki/Hydronym "Hydronym")—i.e. a name linked to the river (Rhône), but perhaps also an oronym of terrain (the *Rocher des Doms*).

The *Auenion* of the 1st century BC was Latinized to *Avennĭo* (or *Avēnĭo*), *-ōnis* in the 1st century and is written *Avinhon* in classic [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") spelling[[15]](#cite_note-15) or *Avignoun* in [Mistralian spelling](/wiki/Mistralian_norm "Mistralian norm").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEXavier_de_FourvièreRupert1902[httpsarchiveorgstreamloupichottresord00xaviuoftpagen88mode1up_62]-16) The inhabitants of the commune are called *avinhonencs* or *avignounen* in both standard Occitan and [Provençal dialect](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Provençal dialect").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Avignon is on the left bank of the [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône") river, a few kilometres above its confluence with the [Durance](/wiki/Durance "Durance"), about 580 km (360 mi) south-east of Paris, 229 km (142 mi) south of [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") and 85 km (53 mi) north-north-west of [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"). On the west it shares a border with the department of [Gard](/wiki/Gard "Gard") and the communes of [Villeneuve-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Villeneuve-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Villeneuve-lès-Avignon") and [Les Angles](/wiki/Les_Angles,_Gard "Les Angles, Gard") and to the south it borders the department of [Bouches-du-Rhône](/wiki/Bouches-du-Rh%C3%B4ne "Bouches-du-Rhône") and the communes of [Barbentane](/wiki/Barbentane "Barbentane"), [Rognonas](/wiki/Rognonas "Rognonas"), [Châteaurenard](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teaurenard "Châteaurenard"), and [Noves](/wiki/Noves "Noves").

The city is in the vicinity of [Orange](/wiki/Orange,_Vaucluse "Orange, Vaucluse") (north), [Carpentras](/wiki/Carpentras "Carpentras") (north-east), [Salon-de-Provence](/wiki/Aix-en-Provence "Aix-en-Provence"), [Aix-en-Provence](/wiki/Aix-en-Provence "Aix-en-Provence"), [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") (all south-east), [Arles](/wiki/Arles "Arles") (south), [Nîmes](/wiki/N%C3%AEmes "Nîmes") and [Montpellier](/wiki/Montpellier "Montpellier") (both south-west). Directly contiguous to the east and north are the communes of [Caumont-sur-Durance](/wiki/Caumont-sur-Durance "Caumont-sur-Durance"), [Morières-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Mori%C3%A8res-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Morières-lès-Avignon"), [Le Pontet](/wiki/Le_Pontet,_Vaucluse "Le Pontet, Vaucluse"), and [Sorgues](/wiki/Sorgues "Sorgues").

### Geology and terrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geology and terrain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/P1280052_-_Avignon.JPG/250px-P1280052_-_Avignon.JPG)](/wiki/File:P1280052_-_Avignon.JPG)

Aerial view of Avignon

The region around Avignon is very rich in [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") which is used for building material. For example, the current [city walls](/wiki/Walls_of_Avignon "Walls of Avignon"), measuring 4,330 metres long, were built with the soft limestone abundant in the region called *mollasse burdigalienne*.[[17]](#cite_note-geologie-17)

Enclosed by the city walls, the *Rocher des Doms* is a limestone elevation of *[Urgonian](/wiki/Urgonian_Limestone "Urgonian Limestone")* type, 35 metres high[[18]](#cite_note-18) (and therefore safe from flooding of the Rhone which it overlooks) and is the original core of the city. Several limestone massifs are present around the commune (the *Massif des Angles*, *Villeneuve-lès-Avignon*, *Alpilles*...) and they are partly the result of the [oceanisation](/wiki/Oceanisation "Oceanisation") of the Ligurian-Provençal basin following the migration of the Sardo-Corsican block.[[17]](#cite_note-geologie-17)

The other significant elevation in the commune is the [Montfavet](/wiki/Montfavet "Montfavet") Hill—a wooded hill in the east of the commune.[[17]](#cite_note-geologie-17)

The [Rhone Valley](/wiki/Rhone_Valley "Rhone Valley") is an old alluvial zone: loose deposits cover much of the ground. It consists of sandy alluvium more or less coloured with pebbles consisting mainly of siliceous rocks. The islands in the Rhone, such as the *Île de la Barthelasse*, were created by the accumulation of alluvial deposits and also by the work of man. The relief is quite low despite the creation of mounds allowing local protection from flooding.[[17]](#cite_note-geologie-17)

In the land around the city there are clay, silt, sand, and limestone present.[[17]](#cite_note-geologie-17)

### Hydrography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hydrography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Vue_des_ponts_d%27Avignon_par_JM_Rosier.JPG/250px-Vue_des_ponts_d%27Avignon_par_JM_Rosier.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vue_des_ponts_d%27Avignon_par_JM_Rosier.JPG)

Bridges on the *Grand [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Pont_d%27Avignon.jpg/250px-Pont_d%27Avignon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pont_d%27Avignon.jpg)

The *[Pont d'Avignon](/wiki/Pont_Saint-B%C3%A9n%C3%A9zet "Pont Saint-Bénézet")* on the *Petit Rhône*. In the background is [Mont Ventoux](/wiki/Mont_Ventoux "Mont Ventoux").

The Rhone passes the western edge of the city, but is divided into two branches: the *Petit Rhône*, or "dead arm", for the part that passes next to Avignon and the *Grand Rhône*, or "live arm", for the western channel which passes [Villeneuve-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Villeneuve-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Villeneuve-lès-Avignon") in the [Gard](/wiki/Gard "Gard") department. The two branches are separated by an island, the Île de la Barthelasse. The southernmost tip of the Île de la Barthelasse once formed of a separated island, the L'Île de Piot.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The banks of the Rhone and the Île de la Barthelasse are often subject to flooding during autumn and March. The publication *Floods in France since the 6th century until today – research and documentation*[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChampion1862-20) by Maurice Champion tells about a number of them (until 1862, the flood of 1856 was one of the largest, which destroyed part of the walls). They have never really stopped as shown by the floods in 1943–1944[[21]](#cite_note-21) and again on 23 January 1955[[22]](#cite_note-22) and remain important today—such as the floods of 2 December 2003.[[23]](#cite_note-23) As a result, a new risk mapping has been developed.

The [Durance](/wiki/Durance "Durance") flows along the southern boundary of the commune into the Rhone and marks the departmental boundary with [Bouches-du-Rhône](/wiki/Bouches-du-Rh%C3%B4ne "Bouches-du-Rhône").[[24]](#cite_note-sandre-24) It is a river that is considered "capricious" and once feared for its floods (it was once called the "3rd scourge of Provence"[[a]](#cite_note-25) as well as for its low water: the Durance has both Alpine and Mediterranean morphology which is unusual.

There are many natural and artificial water lakes in the commune such as the Lake of Saint-Chamand east of the city.

#### Artificial diversions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Artificial diversions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Moulin_Parc_chico_mendes_6.jpg/250px-Moulin_Parc_chico_mendes_6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moulin_Parc_chico_mendes_6.jpg)

A mill on the Vaucluse Canal in the *Pont des 2 eaux* district

There have been many diversions[[25]](#cite_note-26) throughout the course of history, such as feeding the moat surrounding Avignon or irrigating crops.

In the 10th century part of the waters from the *Sorgue d'Entraigues* were diverted and today pass under the walls to enter the city. (See [Sorgue](/wiki/Sorgue "Sorgue")). This watercourse is called the Vaucluse Canal but Avignon people still call it the *Sorgue* or *Sorguette*. It is visible in the city in the *Rue des teinturiers* (street of dyers). It fed the moat around the first defensive walls then fed the moat on the newer eastern city walls (14th century).[[26]](#cite_note-27) In the 13th century (under an Act signed in 1229) part of the waters of the Durance were diverted to increase the water available for the moats starting from Bonpas. This river was later called the *Durançole*. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The *Durançole* fed the western moats of the city and was also used to irrigate crops at Montfavet. In the city, these streams are often hidden beneath the streets and houses and are currently used to collect sewerage. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Hospital Canal (joining the Durançole) and the Crillon Canal (1775) were dug to irrigate the territories of Montfavet, Pontet, and Vedène. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They were divided into numerous "fioles" or "filioles" (in [Provençal](/wiki/Proven%C3%A7al_dialect "Provençal dialect") *filhòlas* or *fiolo*). Similarly, to irrigate the gardens of the wealthy south of Avignon, the Puy Canal was dug (1808). All of these canals took their water from the Durance. These canals were initially used to flood the land, which was very stony, to fertilize them by deposition of silt. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

All of these canals have been used to operate many mills.

### Seismicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Seismicity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Clocher_de_l%27%C3%A9glise_des_Augustins_%28Place_des_Carmes%29_Avignon%2C_by_JM_Rosier.JPG/250px-Clocher_de_l%27%C3%A9glise_des_Augustins_%28Place_des_Carmes%29_Avignon%2C_by_JM_Rosier.JPG)](/wiki/File:Clocher_de_l%27%C3%A9glise_des_Augustins_(Place_des_Carmes)_Avignon,_by_JM_Rosier.JPG)

The leaning bell tower of the Church of the [Augustinians](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians").

Under the new seismic zoning of France defined in Decree No. 2010-1255 of 22 October 2010 concerning the delimitation of the seismicity of the French territory and which entered into force on 1 May 2011, Avignon is located in an area of moderate seismicity. The previous zoning is shown below for reference.

"The cantons of Bonnieux, Apt, Cadenet, Cavaillon, and Pertuis are classified in zone Ib (low risk). All other cantons the Vaucluse department, including Avignon, are classified Ia (very low risk). This zoning is for exceptional seismicity resulting in the destruction of buildings."[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The presence of faults in the limestone substrate shows that significant tectonic shift has caused earthquakes in different geological ages. The last major earthquake of significant magnitude was on 11 June 1909.[[b]](#cite_note-29) It left a visible trace in the centre of the city since the bell tower of the Augustinians, which is surmounted by an ancient [campanile](/wiki/Campanile "Campanile") of wrought iron, located in Rue Carreterie, remained slightly leaning as a result of this earthquake.

Main article: [1909 Provence earthquake](/wiki/1909_Provence_earthquake "1909 Provence earthquake")

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

Avignon has a [hot-summer mediterranean climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer mediterranean climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): **Csa**), though the dry-summer effect is not as strong as coastal locations like [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") due to its more sheltered inland location. The city experiences mild-cool winters and hot summers, with moderate rainfall year-round. The city is often subject to windy weather; the strongest wind is the [mistral](/wiki/Mistral_(wind) "Mistral (wind)"). A medieval Latin proverb said of the city: *Avenie ventosa, sine vento venenosa, cum vento fastidiosa* (Windy Avignon, pest-ridden when there is no wind, wind-pestered when there is).[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMistral1979[httpsgallicabnffrark12148bpt6k74854f203image_196]-30)

#### The mistral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The mistral")]

The prevailing wind is the [mistral](/wiki/Mistral_(wind) "Mistral (wind)") for which the windspeed can be beyond 110 km/h. ## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Administration")]

Avignon is the *[prefecture](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture")* (capital) of [Vaucluse](/wiki/Vaucluse "Vaucluse") *[department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France")* in the [Provence-Alpes-Côte-d'Azur](/wiki/Provence-Alpes-C%C3%B4te-d%27Azur "Provence-Alpes-Côte-d'Azur") region. It forms the core of the [Grand Avignon](/wiki/Grand_Avignon "Grand Avignon") metropolitan area (*[communauté d'agglomération](/wiki/Communaut%C3%A9_d%27agglom%C3%A9ration "Communauté d'agglomération")*), which comprises 15 communes on both sides of the river:[[34]](#cite_note-36)

* [Les Angles](/wiki/Les_Angles,_Gard "Les Angles, Gard"), [Pujaut](/wiki/Pujaut "Pujaut"), [Rochefort-du-Gard](/wiki/Rochefort-du-Gard "Rochefort-du-Gard"), [Sauveterre](/wiki/Sauveterre,_Gard "Sauveterre, Gard"), [Saze](/wiki/Saze "Saze") and [Villeneuve-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Villeneuve-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Villeneuve-lès-Avignon") in the [Gard](/wiki/Gard "Gard") **département**;
* **Avignon**, [Caumont-sur-Durance](/wiki/Caumont-sur-Durance "Caumont-sur-Durance"), [Entraigues-sur-la-Sorgue](/wiki/Entraigues-sur-la-Sorgue "Entraigues-sur-la-Sorgue"), [Jonquerettes](/wiki/Jonquerettes "Jonquerettes"), [Morières-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Mori%C3%A8res-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Morières-lès-Avignon"), [Le Pontet](/wiki/Le_Pontet,_Vaucluse "Le Pontet, Vaucluse"), [Saint-Saturnin-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Saint-Saturnin-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Saint-Saturnin-lès-Avignon"), [Vedène](/wiki/Ved%C3%A8ne "Vedène") and [Velleron](/wiki/Velleron "Velleron") in the Vaucluse **département**.

### Evolution of the borders of the commune

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Evolution of the borders of the commune")]

Avignon absorbed [Montfavet](/wiki/Montfavet "Montfavet") between 1790 and 1794 then ceded [Morières-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Mori%C3%A8res-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Morières-lès-Avignon") in 1870 and [Le Pontet](/wiki/Le_Pontet,_Vaucluse "Le Pontet, Vaucluse") in 1925.[[33]](#cite_note-ehess-35) On 16 May 2007 the commune of [Les Angles](/wiki/Les_Angles,_Gard "Les Angles, Gard") in [Gard](/wiki/Gard "Gard") ceded 13 hectares to Avignon.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Economy")]

Avignon is the seat of the *Chamber of Commerce and Industry of Vaucluse* which manages the Avignon–Caumont Airport and the Avignon-Le Pontet Docks.

Avignon has 7,000 businesses, 1,550 associations, 1,764 shops, and 1,305 service providers.[[37]](#cite_note-mariecarte-39) The urban area has one of the largest catchment areas in Europe with more than 300,000 square metres of retail space and 469 m2 per thousand population against 270 on average in France.[[38]](#cite_note-40) The commercial area of Avignon Nord is one of the largest in Europe.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

The tertiary sector is the most dynamic in the department by far on the basis of the significant production of early fruit and vegetables in Vaucluse, The MIN (Market of National Importance) has become the pivotal hub of commercial activity in the department, taking precedence over other local markets (including that of [Carpentras](/wiki/Carpentras "Carpentras")).

A [Sensitive urban zone](/wiki/Sensitive_urban_zone "Sensitive urban zone") was created for companies wanting to relocate with exemptions from tax and social issues.[[40]](#cite_note-42) It is located south of Avignon between the city walls and the Durance located in the districts of Croix Rouge, Monclar, Saint-Chamand, and La Rocade.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

### Areas of economic activity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Areas of economic activity")]

There are nine main areas of economic activity in Avignon.[[42]](#cite_note-ZAE-44)

The Courtine area is the largest with nearly 300 businesses (of which roughly half are service establishments, one third are shops, and the rest related to industry) and more than 3,600 jobs.[[42]](#cite_note-ZAE-44) The site covers an area of 300 hectares and is located south-west of the city at the [TGV railway station](/wiki/Avignon_TGV_station "Avignon TGV station").

Then comes the Fontcouverte area with a hundred establishments representing a thousand jobs. It is, however, more oriented towards shops than the Courtine area.[[42]](#cite_note-ZAE-44)

The MIN area of Avignon is the Agroparc area[[c]](#cite_note-45) (or "Technopole Agroparc"). The Cristole area is contiguous and both have a little less than a hundred establishments.[[42]](#cite_note-ZAE-44)

Finally, the areas of Castelette, Croix de Noves, Realpanier, and the airport each have fewer than 25 establishments spread between service activities and shops. The area of the Castelette alone represents more than 600 jobs—i.e. 100 more than Cristole.[[42]](#cite_note-ZAE-44)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Four million visitors come annually to visit the city and the region and also for its [Festival d'Avignon](/wiki/Festival_d%27Avignon "Festival d'Avignon").[[37]](#cite_note-mariecarte-39) In 2011 the most visited tourist attraction was the Palais des Papes with 572,972 paying visitors.[[43]](#cite_note-otstats-46) The annual Festival d'Avignon is the most important cultural event in the city. The official festival attracted 135,800 people in 2012.[[43]](#cite_note-otstats-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Viking_Burgundy_%28ship%2C_2000%29_001.jpg/250px-Viking_Burgundy_%28ship%2C_2000%29_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viking_Burgundy_(ship,_2000)_001.jpg)

A cruise boat at the quay at Avignon.

River tourism began in 1994 with three river boat-hotels. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2011 there is a fleet of 21 river boat-hotel vessels, including six sight-seeing boats which are anchored on the quay along the Oulle walkways. In addition, a free shuttle boat connects Avignon to the Île de la Barthelasse and, as of 1987, a harbor master has managed all river traffic.

The commune has been awarded one flower by the *National Council of Towns and Villages in Bloom* in the *Competition of cities and villages in Bloom*.[[44]](#cite_note-47)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Fruits_et_l%C3%A9gumes_aux_Halles.jpg/250px-Fruits_et_l%C3%A9gumes_aux_Halles.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fruits_et_l%C3%A9gumes_aux_Halles.jpg)

Fruit and vegetables at the markets of Avignon

The city is the headquarters of the International Association of the Mediterranean [Tomato](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), the World Council of the [Tomato Industry](/w/index.php?title=Tomato_Industry&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tomato Industry (page does not exist)"), and the Inter-Rhône organisation.

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Industry")]

Only [EDF](/wiki/%C3%89lectricit%C3%A9_de_France "Électricité de France") (Grand Delta) with about 850 employees and *Onet Propreté*[[d]](#cite_note-48) with just over 300 exceed 100 employees.[[45]](#cite_note-Sud_Insee-49)

### Public sector (excluding government)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Public sector (excluding government)")]

The Henri Duffaut hospital, the City of Avignon, and the CHS of Montfavet are the largest employers in the town with about 2,000 employees each. Then comes the General Council of Vaucluse with about 1,300 employees.[[45]](#cite_note-Sud_Insee-49)

### Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Employment")]

In 2017 the unemployment rate was 26.0% while it was 20.7% in 2007.[[46]](#cite_note-complet-50) There are 38,731 people in the Avignon workforce: 102 (0.3%) agricultural workers, 2,194 (5.7%) tradesmen, shopkeepers, and business managers, 5,598 (14.5%) managers and intellectuals, 8,486 (21.9%) middle managers, 11,734 (30.3%) employees, and 9,247 (23.9%) workers.[[46]](#cite_note-complet-50)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Roads")]

Avignon is close to two motorways:

* the **[A7 autoroute](/wiki/A7_autoroute "A7 autoroute")** (E714) is a north–south axis on which there are two exits: ![Exit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Sinnbild_Autobahnausfahrt.svg/40px-Sinnbild_Autobahnausfahrt.svg.png) 23  Avignon-Nord (Northern districts of Avignon, Le Pontet, Carpentras) and ![Exit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Sinnbild_Autobahnausfahrt.svg/40px-Sinnbild_Autobahnausfahrt.svg.png) 24  Avignon-Sud (Southern districts of Avignon, Avignon-Caumont Airport);
* the **[A9 autoroute](/wiki/A9_autoroute "A9 autoroute")** (E15) which branches from the A7 near Orange along a north-east south-west axis towards [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").

The main roads are:

* [Route nationale](/wiki/Route_nationale "Route nationale") N100 which goes west to [Remoulins](/wiki/Remoulins "Remoulins")
* The D225 which goes north towards [Entraigues-sur-la-Sorgue](/wiki/Entraigues-sur-la-Sorgue "Entraigues-sur-la-Sorgue")
* The D62 which goes north-east to [Vedène](/wiki/Ved%C3%A8ne "Vedène")
* The D28 which goes east to [Saint-Saturnin-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Saint-Saturnin-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Saint-Saturnin-lès-Avignon")
* The D901 which goes south-east to [Morières-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Mori%C3%A8res-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Morières-lès-Avignon")
* Route nationale N570 which goes south to [Rognonas](/wiki/Rognonas "Rognonas")

The city has nine paid multi-storey car parks with a total of 7,100 spaces, multi-storey car parks under surveillance with a capacity for 2,050 cars with a free shuttle to the city centre, as well as five other free parking areas with a capacity of 900 cars.[[47]](#cite_note-51)

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Railways")]

Avignon is served by two railway stations: the historic station built in 1860, the *[Gare d'Avignon-Centre](/wiki/Gare_d%27Avignon-Centre "Gare d'Avignon-Centre")*, just outside the city walls, which can accommodate any type of train and, since 2001, the *[Gare d'Avignon TGV](/wiki/Gare_d%27Avignon_TGV "Gare d'Avignon TGV")* in the Courtine district south of the city, on the *[LGV Méditerranée](/wiki/LGV_M%C3%A9diterran%C3%A9e "LGV Méditerranée")* line. Since December 2013 the two stations have been connected by a link line—the *Virgule*. The [Montfavet](/wiki/Montfavet "Montfavet") district, which was formerly a separate commune, also has a station.[[48]](#cite_note-transport-52)

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Airports")]

The [Avignon - Caumont Airport](/wiki/Avignon_-_Caumont_Airport "Avignon - Caumont Airport") on the south-eastern commune border has several international routes to [England](/wiki/England "England"). The major airport in the region with domestic and international scheduled passenger service is the [Marseille Provence Airport](/wiki/Marseille_Provence_Airport "Marseille Provence Airport").

### Water transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Water transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Avignon_-_Navette_Fluviale.JPG/250px-Avignon_-_Navette_Fluviale.JPG)](/wiki/File:Avignon_-_Navette_Fluviale.JPG)

A water taxi departing from its berth

The [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône") has for many centuries been an important means of transportation for the city. River traffic in Avignon has two commercial ports, docking stations for boat cruises, and various riverfront developments. A free shuttle boat has been established between the quay near the city walls and the opposite bank (the île de la Barthelasse).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/StationVelopop.JPG/250px-StationVelopop.JPG)](/wiki/File:StationVelopop.JPG)

A *vélopop* cycle station next to the city walls

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Public transport")]

Tecelys[[49]](#cite_note-53) via the [*Orizo network*](/wiki/Transports_en_commun_d%27Avignon "Transports en commun d'Avignon") is the public transport operator for the commune of Avignon and its surrounding suburbs. Tecelys operates [bus](/wiki/Bus "Bus") services (including [BRT](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") named *Chron'hop*[[50]](#cite_note-54)), as well as [bike sharing](/wiki/Bike_sharing "Bike sharing") and [car pooling](/wiki/Car_pooling "Car pooling") services. The [Avignon tramway](/wiki/Avignon_tramway "Avignon tramway") began operation in October 2019.[[51]](#cite_note-55)[[52]](#cite_note-56) The city previously had a tram system between 1901 and 1932.

### Bicycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Bicycles")]

Avignon has 110 km (68 mi) of bicycle paths.[[37]](#cite_note-mariecarte-39) In 2009 the TCRA (former public transport operator) introduced a [bicycle sharing system](/wiki/Bicycle_sharing_system "Bicycle sharing system") called the *[Vélopop'](/wiki/V%C3%A9lopop%27 "Vélopop'")*.[[53]](#cite_note-57)

## Cultural heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Cultural heritage")]

Avignon has a very large number of sites and buildings (177) that are registered as historical monuments.[[54]](#cite_note-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Avignon_shops.jpg/250px-Avignon_shops.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avignon_shops.jpg)

Rue de la République, the city's central boulevard

In the part of the city within the walls the buildings are old but in most areas they have been restored or reconstructed (such as the post office and the Lycée Frédéric Mistral).[[55]](#cite_note-59) The buildings along the main street, Rue de la République, date from the [Second Empire (1852–70)](/wiki/Second_Empire_(architecture) "Second Empire (architecture)") with [Haussmann](/wiki/Georges-Eug%C3%A8ne_Haussmann "Georges-Eugène Haussmann") façades and amenities around Place de l'Horloge (the central square), the neoclassical city hall, and the theatre district.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Avignon_statues.jpg/250px-Avignon_statues.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avignon_statues.jpg)

Statues gaze over the *Place de l'Horloge* in the town centre

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Avignon_facades.jpg/250px-Avignon_facades.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avignon_facades.jpg)

Paintings on the façades of buildings in the town centre

The commune houses more than 500 historical objects, many of which are religious.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Avignon Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Avignon Festival")]

Main article: [Festival d'Avignon](/wiki/Festival_d%27Avignon "Festival d'Avignon")

A theatre festival is held annually in Avignon. Founded in 1947, the [Festival d'Avignon](/wiki/Festival_d%27Avignon "Festival d'Avignon") (Avignon Festival) comprises traditional theatrical events as well as other art forms such as dance, music, and cinema, making use of the town's historical monuments. Every summer approximately 100,000 people attend the festival.[[79]](#cite_note-83) There are really two festivals that take place: the more formal "Festival In", which presents plays inside the Palace of the Popes and the more bohemian "Festival Off", which is known for its presentation of largely undiscovered plays and street performances.

Avignon festival was founded by Jean Vilar. This cultural initiative brought, year after year, a major economic boost to the city and to the region of Provence. Indeed, the tourists visiting Avignon during the month of July usually take benefit of their presence to go to the smaller villages around, to discover the local food, local wines, touristic activities, learn some French.

### International Congress Centre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: International Congress Centre")]

The centre was created in 1976 within the premises of the Palace of the Popes and hosts many events throughout the entire year. The Congress Centre, designed for conventions, seminars, and meetings for 10 to 550 persons, now occupies two wings of the Popes' Palace.[[80]](#cite_note-84)

### "Sur le Pont d'Avignon"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: \"Sur le Pont d'Avignon\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Avignon_Panorama.jpg/960px-Avignon_Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avignon_Panorama.jpg "View over the Rhône looking northeast, with the Pont Saint-Bénézet or \"Pont d'Avignon\" at left")

View over the Rhône looking northeast, with the [Pont Saint-Bénézet](/wiki/Pont_Saint-B%C3%A9n%C3%A9zet "Pont Saint-Bénézet") or "Pont d'Avignon" at left

Avignon is commemorated by the French song '[Sur le Pont d'Avignon](/wiki/Sur_le_Pont_d%27Avignon "Sur le Pont d'Avignon")' ('On the bridge of Avignon'), which describes [folk dancing](/wiki/Folk_dance "Folk dance"). The song dates from the mid-19th century when [Adolphe Adam](/wiki/Adolphe_Adam "Adolphe Adam") included it in the [Opéra comique](/wiki/Op%C3%A9ra_comique "Opéra comique") *Le Sourd ou l'Auberge Pleine*, which was first performed in Paris in 1853. The opera was an adaptation of the 1790 comedy by [Desforges](/wiki/Pierre_Jean_Baptiste_Choudard_Desforges "Pierre Jean Baptiste Choudard Desforges").[[81]](#cite_note-85)

The bridge of the song is the [Pont Saint-Bénézet](/wiki/Pont_Saint-B%C3%A9n%C3%A9zet "Pont Saint-Bénézet") over the [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône"), of which only four arches (out of the initial 22) now remain. A bridge across the Rhone was built between 1171 and 1185, with a length of some 900 metres (3,000 ft), but was destroyed during the [siege of Avignon](/wiki/Siege_of_Avignon_(1226) "Siege of Avignon (1226)") by [Louis VIII of France](/wiki/Louis_VIII_of_France "Louis VIII of France") in 1226. It was rebuilt but suffered frequent collapses during floods and had to be continually repaired. Several arches were already missing (and spanned by wooden sections) before the remainder was abandoned in 1669.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERouquette1974219–229-86)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Sport")]

[Sporting Olympique Avignon](/wiki/Sporting_Olympique_Avignon "Sporting Olympique Avignon") is the local [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football team. During the 20th century it produced a number of French international representative players.

[AC Arles-Avignon](/wiki/AC_Arles-Avignon "AC Arles-Avignon") was a professional association football team. They competed in [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"), after the 2010–2011 season competing in Ligue 1 and being relegated back down the following season and ultimately folding in 2016. They played at the [Parc des Sports](/wiki/Parc_des_Sports_(Avignon) "Parc des Sports (Avignon)"), which has a capacity of just over 17,000.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Education")]

The schools within the commune of Avignon are administered by the Académie d'Aix-Marseille. There are 26 state nursery schools (*Écoles maternelles*) for children up to 6, and 32 state primary schools (*Écoles élémentaires*) up to 11. There are also 4 private schools.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

### University of Avignon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: University of Avignon")]

See also: [Medieval university](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") and [List of medieval universities](/wiki/List_of_medieval_universities "List of medieval universities")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/UniversiteAvignon-Entree.jpg/250px-UniversiteAvignon-Entree.jpg)](/wiki/File:UniversiteAvignon-Entree.jpg)

Entrance to the main university building. This 18th century portico was once the entrance to the Hôpital Sainte-Marthe.

#### University before the Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: University before the Revolution")]

The medieval University of Avignon, formed from the existing schools of the city, was formally constituted in 1303 by [Boniface VIII](/wiki/Boniface_VIII "Boniface VIII") in a [Papal Bull](/wiki/Papal_Bull "Papal Bull"). Boniface VIII and [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Naples "Charles II of Naples") of Naples were the first great protectors and benefactors to the university. The Law department was the most important department covering both civil and [ecclesiastical law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"). The law department existed nearly exclusively for some time after the university's formation and remained its most important department throughout its existence.[[84]](#cite_note-ceuni-88)

In 1413 [Pope John XXIII](/wiki/Antipope_John_XXIII "Antipope John XXIII") founded the university's department of theology, which for quite some time had only a few students. It was not until the 16th and 17th centuries that the school developed a department of medicine. The [bishop of Avignon](/wiki/Bishop_of_Avignon "Bishop of Avignon") was chancellor of the university from 1303 to 1475. After 1475 the bishop became an archbishop but remained chancellor of the university. The papal vice-legate, generally a bishop, represented the civil power (in this case the pope) and was chiefly a judicial officer who ranked higher than the Primicerius (Rector).[[84]](#cite_note-ceuni-88)

The Primicerius was elected by the Doctors of Law. In 1503 the Doctors of Law had 4 Theologians and in 1784 two [Doctors of Medicine](/wiki/Doctor_of_Medicine "Doctor of Medicine") added to their ranks. Since the Pope was the spiritual head and, after 1348, the temporal ruler of Avignon, he was able to have a great deal of influence in all university affairs. In 1413 [John XXIII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXIII "Pope John XXIII") granted the university extensive special privileges, such as university jurisdiction and [tax exempt status](/wiki/Tax_exemption "Tax exemption"). Political, geographical, and educational circumstances in the latter part of the university's existence caused it to seek favour from Paris rather than Rome for protection. During the chaos of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") the university started to gradually disappear and, in 1792, the university was abandoned and closed.[[84]](#cite_note-ceuni-88)

#### Modern university

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Avignon&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Modern university")]

A university annex of the *Faculté des Sciences d'Aix-Marseille* was opened in Avignon in 1963. Over the next 20 years various changes were made to the provision of tertiary education in the town until finally in 1984 the *Université d'Avignon et des Pays de Vaucluse* was created. This was nearly 200 years after the demise of the original Avignon university.[[85]](#cite_note-89) The main campus lies to the east of the city centre within the city walls. The university occupies the 18th century buildings of the *Hôpital Sainte-Marthe*. The main building has an elegant façade with a central [portico](/wiki/Portico "Portico"). The right hand side was designed by [Jean-Baptiste Franque](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Franque "Jean-Baptiste Franque") and built between 1743 and 1745. Franque was assisted by his son François in the design of the portico. The hospital moved out in the 1980s and, after major works, the building opened for students in 1997.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVingtainAujard-Catot2001123–124-90)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGirard1958306–307-91) In 2009–2010 there were 7,125 students registered at the university.[[88]](#cite_note-92)
